ontology.foodex2_description: Bulgur wheat, cooked — mapped to FoodEx2 composite grain product; closest defensible code under processed cereal grain category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for boiled bulgur; A000F (wheat-based grain product) used as closest defensible anchor. Reviewer should confirm against EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for boiled bulgur wheat as a food product; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of boiled bulgur is approximately 182 g (1 cup cooked), providing around 151 kcal, 5.6 g protein, 33.8 g carbohydrate, and 8.2 g dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Bulgur, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20014)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for bulgur, cooked (NDB 20014 / FDC ID 170285) used as primary nutrient anchor. Singapore HPB values for energy (83 kcal), protein (3.08 g), fat (0.24 g), carbohydrate (18.58 g), sugar (0.1 g), sodium (5 mg), dietary fibre (4.5 g), potassium (68 mg), calcium (10 mg), iron (0.96 mg), and cholesterol (0 mg) are preserved from the input as they are consistent with HPB Singapore database (canonical source for Singapore food records). USDA SR Legacy used to supplement saturated fat and additional micronutrient fields not present in HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Bulgur wheat GI of 46 (±5) is documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and in Atkinson et al. (2008) International tables of GI and GL values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. This is an exact match for boiled bulgur wheat. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (46 × 18.58) / 100 = 8.55 ≈ 8.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5 mg/100g) and high dietary fibre (4.5 g/100g) supporting vascular health
hypertension.guidance: Boiled bulgur is appropriate for hypertensive individuals; its sodium content of 5 mg/100g is well within DASH diet limits, and its 4.5 g/100g dietary fibre contributes to blood pressure reduction through improved endothelial function and reduced arterial stiffness.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 46) and high dietary fibre (4.5 g/100g) attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled bulgur has a low GI of 46 and provides 4.5 g/100g dietary fibre, both of which slow glucose absorption and reduce postprandial glycaemic excursions; individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor total carbohydrate intake (18.6 g/100g) and limit portions to 150–200 g per meal in accordance with ADA carbohydrate-counting guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.04 g/100g), zero trans fat, and high dietary fibre supporting LDL reduction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled bulgur is a cardioprotective whole grain with negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100g), zero trans fat, and 4.5 g/100g soluble and insoluble fibre that supports LDL cholesterol reduction; regular whole-grain consumption is associated with reduced cardiovascular event risk per AHA dietary guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome-mediated immune regulation and negligible saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled bulgur provides 4.5 g/100g dietary fibre that supports gut microbiome diversity and immune modulation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; its negligible saturated fat and absence of trans fat are consistent with anti-inflammatory dietary patterns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled bulgur is a whole-grain cereal product made from parboiled, dried, and cracked durum wheat (Triticum durum) that has been rehydrated by boiling or soaking in hot water. The result is a light brown, tender grain with a slightly chewy texture and a mild, nutty flavour. Bulgur is a staple of Middle Eastern and Mediterranean cuisines, widely used in dishes such as tabbouleh, kibbeh, and pilaf, and is increasingly consumed globally as a nutritious whole-grain alternative to rice or couscous. Per 100 g cooked, boiled bulgur provides approximately 83 kcal, 3.1 g protein, 0.24 g fat, 18.6 g carbohydrate, 4.5 g dietary fibre, and only 5 mg sodium. Its low glycemic index of 46 — attributable to its intact whole-grain structure and high fibre content — makes it a preferred grain for blood glucose management in individuals with type 2 diabetes. The high fibre content also supports cardiovascular health by contributing to LDL cholesterol reduction and improved gut microbiome diversity. Boiled bulgur is naturally vegan, plant-based, low in sodium, and low in saturated fat, making it suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. It contains gluten and is not appropriate for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. It is a good source of B vitamins including thiamine, niacin, and folate, as well as minerals such as magnesium, phosphorus, and zinc.
common_user_queries: Is bulgur wheat high in fibre?
Can I eat bulgur if I have high blood pressure?
Is bulgur suitable for a vegan diet?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced from the Singapore HPB database (canonical primary source for Singapore food records, confidence 0.95 per input metadata). Supplementary micronutrient values are cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20014, bulgur cooked), which is an exact match. GI value of 46 is confirmed in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) peer-reviewed publication. All values are internally consistent and plausible for boiled bulgur wheat.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) are preserved from the HPB Singapore input record (confidence 0.95). 2. Saturated fat (0.04 g), monounsaturated fat (0.03 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.10 g), trans fat (0.0 g), and micronutrient values (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, B6, folate, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 170285 (bulgur, cooked). 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae and vitamin_e_mg are set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore database for this entry, and USDA SR Legacy reports negligible/trace amounts that are not consistently documented across sources; set to -1 pending confirmation. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — bulgur is a plant-based grain product containing no cholesterol, confirmed by both HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero — bulgur wheat contains no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed by USDA SR Legacy. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based grain product contains no vitamin B12 or vitamin D. 7. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: naturally zero for cooked bulgur wheat per USDA SR Legacy. 8. Glycemic load per serving calculated on a per-100g basis: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (46 × 18.58) / 100 = 8.55 ≈ 8.6. 9. FoodEx2 code A000F used as closest_parent match for a wheat-based cooked grain product; reviewer should verify against EFSA FoodEx2 revision 2 catalogue. 10. Boiled bulgur contains gluten (wheat) and is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. 11. Local-language names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as general multilingual enrichment; bulgur is not a Singaporean dish by origin but names are provided for retrieval completeness.
